utterances: Pickford auctioned off one of her curls for the World War I effort.
It went for $15,000.
Eventually, tired of playing little girls, she cut the curls off.
You would've thought she'd murdered the American eagle.
That's how much fans all over the world adored Pickford's little-girl image.
They needed that image of sweetness and innocence.
And at just 5 feet tall, she portrayed it so convincingly without speaking a single word that audiences could hear.
In fact, when movies began to talk in 1927, Doe Mayer says Mary disapproved.
She thought that sound was a terrible idea, that movies were an art in the silent form.
And she said it was like putting lipstick on the Venus de Milo.
(SOUNDBITE OF FILM, ""COQUETTE"")
(As Norma Besant) Well, I told you, I told you he made love to me.
(As character) Yes, and he was gentle and tender.
(As Norma Besant) No, no, no.
(As character) Are you sure?
(As Norma Besant) No.
Her first talkie was ""Coquette,"" 1929 - no curls, an unsuitable suitor, a murder, a new Mary and the Oscar.
Four years later, she gave up acting but continued as a major force in the industry.
Look at the ceiling in here - just soaring.
A theater in downtown LA is a marvel thanks to Mary.
In 1919, she and three other big movie pioneers - Charlie Chaplin, Douglas Fairbanks - she married him the next year - and director D.W.
Griffith - founded United Artists to make and distribute their own films and rake in the profits.
They built a big office building on LA's Broadway.
Today, it's the Ace Hotel.
The adjacent theater - ornate Spanish Gothic with sculptures and lobby fountains and murals - was for showing their films.
This is nice decoration, too, with...
Mary supervised every inch.
I mean, the toilets in the ladies' room are pink.
It was a movie palace.
And they called the actors the kings and queens of the industry.
Before United Artists, Queen Mary had launched her own royal playground, the Pickford-Fairbanks Studio.
She hired directors, writers, cast and crew, OK'd the scripts.
She would be the envy of every one of today's industry women.
How did she get that power a century ago?
It started with her popularity as a star.
She leveraged her box office in a way that most actors didn't.
She saw herself as a franchise, and she demanded more, and more and more.
Remember, this is a woman who in 1909 is making $10 a day.
And in 1919, she's making $250,000 a film.
Again, Cari Beauchamp.
Ten years - poof.
But she didn't just accept being a star.
She took that power and became a producer, became a company owner, and became an owner of the theaters themselves and of a studio lot.
A lot of information.

We had last spring, the Associated Press succeeded in getting the basic information, the basic transcripts out of DOD, but the names and the nationalities weren't attached, and so you had a whole lot of words about a lot of anonymous men.
Now that you have names and nationalities written in there, these people begin to become more real.
So they removed the redactions.
Right.
You can actually see who they're talking about.
Now, we should say this is not actually a complete picture.
The documents only provide information about half of the detainees, and classified evidence is excluded as well.
But based on what you've seen, what do we now know about this very secretive prison?

Based upon both these transcripts and an additional set of documents that DOD has been forced to file in individual court cases, we know that most of Guantanamo Bay was not captured on a battlefield, as you would traditionally define it.
In fact, most of Guantanamo Bay does not appear to have been captured in Afghanistan.
Most of them were caught in Pakistan by third parties and then turned over to the United States.
Furthermore, most of the men are not accused of having taken part in hostilities against the United States.
They are said instead to be associated with either al-Qaida or the Taliban, but that association is sometimes very strong and sometimes very weak.
Can you give us an example of one case where the association seems to be strong and one where it would perhaps appear to be more tenuous?
There is one individual who was picked up within Pakistan during what looks like a U.S.-guided raid who appears to be an al-Qaida financier.
There is information and references to documents and money that he was intimately involved in.
There is another example of a young man who was picked up in Pakistan for whom his only association came from the fact that he stayed within a guesthouse that was associated with the Taliban.
He had no concrete ties to the Taliban regime, but he stayed in a house in which other individuals in the house were with the Taliban.
So the process reveals something about the tribunal but also about the detainees.
In some cases, the arguments that they try to make that they should not be held as enemy combatants.
In other cases, the arguments they make saying yes, I absolutely am an enemy combatant and proud of that fact.
You see both cases.
Yes.

There's one individual, for example, who, when he began his tribunal, he began chanting in Arabic, and the tribunal president asked him to please stop chanting, and he continued doing so anyway, and so they stopped the proceeding and removed him from the courtroom.
Once he was gone, the tribunal reconvened to ask the translator what was the detainee chanting.
And he'd been chanting, ""May God grant me the strength to kill the infidels.
But there are other cases in which the detainees say, no, this isn't right.
For example, there is a Saudi who is accused of, among other things, having admitted that he is a terrorist.
And you're just paging through the actual transcript there.
Right.
This is the officer speaking who is helping this detainee present himself.
They told me that the statement that he had admitted he was a terrorist was taken out of context.
They stated to me that his admission was made at a time when he was frustrated and extremely mad and being sarcastic.
The detainee threw his hands up and said, 'All right, you got me.
I'm a terrorist.'
So there's a bit of sarcasm there.
Yes, there was a bit of sarcasm which was made during the interrogation.
But when the government recorder later was presenting the evidence against the detainee, this sarcastic admission was written down as proof that he is indeed an enemy combatant.
And so his interrogators were so upset that they sought out an officer to explain that's not the case.
And this is all documented within the transcript of this detainee's proceeding.
Now the Pentagon has released these documents, has not yet commented on them, but I imagine that the U.S.
military would say it's not surprising that many of the detainees would deny having any connection to al-Qaida.
They'd probably note that American prisons are full of prisoners who claim to be innocent.
That's absolutely correct.
They say that many of the detainees in Guantanamo Bay are trained to lie.
And certainly there are cases throughout the transcripts of individuals who claimed one story and then later another story came to light that wasn't nearly as favorable.
They had been lying in the beginning.

But on the flipside, after four years, if the only evidence you have against somebody is that he stayed in a Taliban house, or he admitted he was a terrorist during a sarcastic interview, how long do you get to hold onto the person to find out if he's telling the truth or if he's lying?
You've been trying to get your hands on this information for some time.
Now that these documents have been released through this Freedom of Information request, what's the most important information that you see in these papers?
Simply the possibility of pulling together a more comprehensive picture of who's there.
A lot of information has come out of Guantanamo in drips and drabs, individual stories here and there brought out by the individual lawyers, brought out by reporters getting access to documents.
With these transcripts, together with the court records, we can actually pull together much more comprehensive information.
So instead of having anecdotes about individuals, we can start to say, and this is what the entirety of the Bay looks like.
Corine, thank you so much for coming in to talk to us.
Thank you for having me.
Corine Hegland is a reporter for The National Journal.
You can see the Guantanamo detainee documents for yourself at our website, NPR.org.
In spite of all the advancement in law enforcement in recent decades, many murders still go unsolved.
Commentator Stacy Horn says that right now in New York you have a 40 percent chance of getting away with murder.
It's not just New York.
Statistics show that every year more than a third of the murders in America go unsolved, and that's been true for decades.
It's especially dramatic in New York City, where the number of murders has dropped 70 percent.
But even as the number of murders goes down, the percentage of unsolved murders has been slowly creeping up and they're going up everywhere.
The police are getting better at preventing murder, but not at solving it.
A majority of Republicans agree with that, which has helped McCain so far.
But he still faces a challenge from Democrats and independents who support a quick troop withdrawal.
Mc Cain says he wants the troops to come home too, but with honor that was denied to so many veterans of Vietnam.
I know we're divided, and I know we're saddened, but I also am proud to say that no American is divided in their support of the brave young men and women who are serving in the military today.
Earlier today, McCain dismissed Obama's comment that the war in Iraq was misguided, saying, that's history.
But McCain himself is a student of history.
In his shadow of Vietnam speech in 1994, McCain told the Marine graduates: I pray that if the time comes for you to answer the call to arms, the battle will be necessary and the field well chosen.
Scott Horsley, NPR News, Houston.
